Title: The faces of quantum
URL: http://chicagoreader.com/news/explainer/quantum-computing-campus-tenants-iqmp-illinois/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T16:19:08Z
Full Content:
This story is part of Quantum Crossroads, a series examining the impact of the quantum computing megadevelopment in South Chicago. The push to transform Chicago into a world leader in quantum computing research and development has been yearslong and required collaboration among local, state, and federal governing bodies, universities, tech companies, and nonprofits. Now, Chicago real estate developers Related Midwest and CRG have begun construction on a state-of-the-art quantum computing campus on the former U.S. Steel South Works site in South Chicago, with plans to finish the first few buildings in 2027. Local organizers say many residents who live closest to the development still know very little about the controversial project, known as the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park (IQMP), coming to their neighborhood, and are concerned about its potential to displace residents, its ties to the military, its impact on the environment, and whether local residents will benefit from jobs it creates. To learn more, the Reader asked major players involved in the project about their role in the development, their research in South Chicago, and how quantum technology could be used in the future. We’ve compiled their responses, along with other publicly available information, below. The IQMP—a quantum computing research and development campus in South Chicago—is a partnership between various public and private actors, including representatives from local universities. IQMP’s operations are managed by a subsidiary of the University of Illinois (UI), also known as a university-related organization. UROs are legal entities that universities use to start companies, purchase real estate, and form alumni associations. In this case, the company is owned by the UI’s board of trustees and run by a board of managers housed within the UI system. The IQMP’s CEO, UI professor Harley T. Johnson, did not address a question from the Reader asking him to outline the university’s research role at the park versus its leadership role. Instead, he spoke to the development as a whole, which he said “brings together companies, researchers, suppliers, end users, and other partners to facilitate the development and commercialization of quantum computers.” The Discovery Partners Institute—a tech hub in downtown Chicago run by the University of Illinois Urbana–Champaign’s (UIUC) Grainger College of Engineering—is one of three “On-Ramp facilities” where future tenants of the IQMP have already begun their research and development work. The UI system is likely to have researchers working at the IQMP in future years, as the Grainger College of Engineering is home to half of the state’s quantum professors, including more than 65 scientists and 170 graduate students, according to a 2024 university blog post. Brian L. DeMarco, a UIUC physics professor, now serves as the IQMP’s director and chief technology officer. Johnson and DeMarco will also play leadership roles in the Illinois Quantum Proving Ground, which will monitor and validate the progress of quantum computing algorithms and hardware developed by researchers at the park. The Quantum Proving Ground is funded with $140 million from the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD), with the state of Illinois matching the investment. The UI board of trustees has 13 members, 11 of whom vote, and nine of whom are appointed by the governor. The body oversees the university system’s campuses in Urbana–Champaign, Chicago, and Springfield, as well as other university-owned assets. Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park LLC, the company spun up by UI, will be managed by a 14-person board of managers. Wilbur Milhouse III, CEO of Milhouse Engineering and Construction, who Governor J.B. Pritzker appointed to the UI board of trustees in 2023, is the company’s board chair. Johnson, a professor of the Grainger College of Engineering, was appointed the IQMP’s inaugural executive director and CEO by the board of managers in September 2024. Johnson has worked with the university since 2001. Other members of the IQMP board of managers include: The UI system’s Discovery Partners Institute (DPI) was announced in 2017 when then governor Bruce Rauner asked the state legislature for $500 million to start a research and innovation institute to keep young tech professionals from leaving the state for jobs. Pritzker recommitted to funding the institute in February 2020. Later that year, the Pritzker Foundation gave $10 million to DPI to create the Pritzker Tech Talent Labs, a program to develop a more diverse local field of prospective tech employees. The DPI facility was initially planned for a large South Loop development site known as the 78 before the university changed its focus to Pritzker’s quantum computing project in October 2024, according to the Chicago Sun-Times. Pulling out of the South Loop project cost taxpayers more than $30 million. In 2026, the university invested an additional $23.75 million in the institute’s new West Loop headquarters, where tenants of the quantum computing campus are getting a head start on research they’ll be doing in South Chicago. Johnson said the quantum computing campus will create “hundreds of jobs,” including cryogenic plant technicians, human resources professionals, and security personnel. He pointed to a Chicago Quantum Exchange report that found about two-thirds of all quantum-related industry jobs are open to people with a bachelor’s degree or less. Back to top The University of Chicago Science Incubator at Hyde Park Labs will serve as another of the IQMP’s three on-ramp facilities for tenants to start their research and development work in Chicago as construction on the actual campus in South Chicago continues over the next few years. The university will provide companies such as Quantum Machines with lab space, equipment, and “innovation programming” provided by the Polsky Center for Entrepreneurship and Innovation, according to a March press release. The Polsky Center, which powers the incubator, was created to help U. Chicago faculty commercialize their research and ideas. The Polsky Center also helped start Duality, a quantum start-up accelerator program, and has been working closely with the state of Illinois and IBM on the company’s new ​​National Quantum Algorithm Center. Until the IQMP campus is ready, the center will operate out of U. Chicago and the Discovery Partners Institute. IBM’s modular quantum computer will be housed at Hyde Park Labs, according to a U. Chicago press release. In response to questions from the Reader about the university’s role in IQMP research and operations, U. Chicago public affairs director Gerald McSwiggan instead highlighted initiatives designed to improve STEM opportunities at local high schools and the City Colleges of Chicago to prepare young people for jobs in the quantum industry. “While UChicago does not currently have an official role in development of the IQMP, the University is working with leaders of that project and other partners to help enable residents of the Chicago region, especially the South Side, to benefit from the quantum economy,” McSwiggan wrote in an emailed statement. He declined to answer questions about the U. Chicago Science Incubator’s role in on-ramping IQMP tenants, instead directing the Reader to a university press release. Nadya Mason, dean of U. Chicago’s Pritzker School for Molecular Engineering, holds a seat on the IQMP’s board of managers. “The University of Chicago is proud to play a leading role in building the quantum economy—advancing breakthrough science, supporting startups and shaping technologies that will power everything from the highest-resolution sensors to next-generation computing,” Mason said in a July 2025 press release about the ongoing work with IBM. The Pritzker Foundation donated a total of $100 million to establish U. Chicago’s Pritzker School for Molecular Engineering in 2019—the university’s first new school in 30 years. The school also received early support from the Argonne National Laboratory, a federally funded research and development center in Lemont, managed by UChicago Argonne LLC for the U.S. Department of Energy. The Polsky Center at U. Chicago is supported by a mix of university funds, philanthropic donations, and private partnerships with companies like JPMorgan Chase. It was established in 1998 and expanded in 2016 with $50 million in funding from Michael Polsky, a university trustee and alumnus of the Booth School of Business. Polsky is also the founder and executive chairman of an energy company called Invenergy. Duality, the accelerator program for quantum computing start-ups, was founded with a $20 million commitment from U. Chicago as part of the Polsky Center’s Deep Tech Ventures, a program designed to fund tech that the private-sector investors may be more hesitant to back. This commitment from the university was matched by a call for $25 million in private investment, according to Crain’s Chicago Business. U. Chicago has been working to create pathways to prepare local youth for careers in “high-tech jobs” like quantum, McSwiggan said in a statement to the Reader. These efforts include a program to bring quantum concepts into high school classrooms, an annual South Side Science Festival, internship programs including the After School Matters STEM Lab Internship and the Youth Internship Program, and summer programs in quantum science and engineering at the City Colleges of Chicago. Back to top PsiQuantum, a quantum computing company started in 2016, is the IQMP’s first and main tenant, according to a press release from the governor’s office. The company seeks to build the U.S.’s first fault-tolerant, “utility-scale” quantum computer, slated for completion in 2028, according to a PsiQuantum spokesperson. If successful, the computer will run as efficiently as a traditional computer yet perform far more complex calculations. It is also planning to develop a quantum computer in Brisbane, Australia, scheduled for completion in 2027. However, a mandatory public outreach period has delayed the Brisbane project, set to begin in 2025, by more than a year, according to a February Nundah News report. Its quantum computer would be much larger in scale than those proposed by other companies, as it would operate using one million quantum bits or “qubits”—the fundamental, physical units that power quantum computers. PsiQuantum’s qubits are made up of single photons, or particles of light, that its researchers put onto semiconductor chips mass-manufactured by its partner GlobalFoundries, a semiconductor manufacturer based in Malta, New York. Read more: What is quantum computing? At a March 2025 technology conference, Pete Shadbolt, PsiQuantum’s chief scientific officer, said that the approach gives the company “profound advantages” in overcoming long-standing industry challenges. “That leverage has put us in this position where we’re now breaking ground in the next few months on very large-scale, data-center-like quantum computers in Australia and in Chicago,” Shadbolt said. Like other quantum computing companies, PsiQuantum has cited a number of potential applications for the technology, including food production, pharmaceuticals, energy, and materials design, according to a PsiQuantum spokesperson. PsiQuantum has also received more than $33.3 million in federal contracts from the U.S. Department of Defense to research how its technology could be used for defense and national security purposes. Nicole Holzmann, a quantum solutions manager for the company based in Germany, explained in a September 2025 presentation for computer science and defense professionals how PsiQuantum’s technology could be used by militaries in the coming years. Holzmann pointed to the technology’s potential to access encrypted digital information, plan logistics of military operations, develop new medications for gunshot wounds, and make aircraft and vehicles more resistant to corrosion. In November 2025, PsiQuantum announced a new strategic partnership with Lockheed Martin, one of the nation’s largest manufacturers of weapons and aerospace technology, to “accelerate the development of quantum computing applications in aerospace and defense.” In the contract, PsiQuantum agreed to develop quantum algorithms for “real-world aerospace and defense applications for the U.S. government and allies” as part of its work at facilities such as the one in South Chicago. Shadbolt holds a PhD in experimental photonic quantum computing from the University of Bristol. In 2016, while he was a postdoctoral researcher at Imperial College London, Shadbolt and three of his professors quit their jobs and moved to Palo Alto, California, to found PsiQuantum with a singular mission “to build and deploy the first useful quantum computers.” The company’s other three founders are executive board chairperson Jeremy O’Brien, chief architect Terry Rudolph, and chief technology officer Mark Thompson. O’Brien was the company’s CEO untilhe was replaced in February by interim CEO Victor Peng, former president of semiconductor manufacturer AMD. In January, PsiQuantum added three members to its government advisory board, which aids the company in engaging with governments and other public partners. Three of four board members have backgrounds in national security: The Department of Defense awarded PsiQuantum a $22.5 million contract in 2022, according to federal procurement data. The company worked with Democratic U.S. Senate majority leader Chuck Schumer to secure $25 million in federal funding in 2022 to support a collaboration among PsiQuantum, GlobalFoundries, and the Air Force Research Laboratory to develop quantum photonic chips. These computer chips use single photons—tiny individual particles of light—to help quantum computers process information, according to a 2022 report by Quantum Insider. PsiQuantum received another $10.8 million contract from the DoD in March 2025. The company has also received $200 million in Illinois tax incentives, capital grants, a low-interest loan, workforce development assistance, and other incentives, according to a 2024 press release from Pritzker’s office. In a statement, a company spokesperson told the Reader, “PsiQuantum is currently participating in the most advanced stage of the federal government’s Quantum Benchmarking Initiative, which is focused on the technical validation and codesign of an industrially useful quantum computer with potentially transformative economic impact.” PsiQuantum’s partnership with the Air Force Research Laboratory in New York “allows the AFRL to draw from PsiQuantum’s leadership in silicon photonic chips for its own innovation,” the spokesperson said. PsiQuantum raised $1 billion from private investors in its latest fundraising round, which closed in September, bringing the company’s total value to an estimated $7 billion, according to company announcements. The fundraising push was led by its three largest investors: BlackRock, the world’s largest investment company; British investment firm Baillie Gifford; and global investment company Temasek. Other private investors include NVentures, the venture capital arm of tech company NVIDIA; Microsoft’s venture capital fund, M12; and Counterpoint Global, the investment arm of financial services firm Morgan Stanley. PsiQuantum has also received funding from Founders Fund, a venture capital fund cofounded by Peter Thiel of Palantir, and 1789 Capital, a venture capital firm committed to funding “the next chapter of American exceptionalism,” of which Donald Trump Jr. is a partner. A PsiQuantum spokesperson told the Reader the company would create at least 150 jobs, “including a broad range of different backgrounds and skills, including technicians, engineers, scientists, software developers, and other operations and business roles.” By 2029, the company has promised to create 154 full-time positions that pay at least 120 percent of the average wage for employees in the same job classification in Cook County in order to remain eligible for tax incentives, according to PsiQuantum’s agreement with the state. Back to top The U.S. created the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency after the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite to orbit earth, in 1957. DARPA was intended to make high-risk investments in technologies that could prove useful to the U.S. military. According to its website, the agency “stands as our nation’s commitment to never again face a strategic technical surprise.” At the IQMP, DARPA will establish its Quantum Proving Ground, the next phase of its Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. The “multi-phase, multi-year” partnership with companies—including both PsiQuantum and another IQMP tenant, Diraq—supports the development of a commercially viable quantum computer by 2033. The Quantum Proving Ground will build on this work over the next five years in partnership with IQMP tenants. DARPA and other federal agencies have committed $140 million in federal funds to the program, and the state of Illinois has agreed to match that investment, according to Pritzker’s office. The Quantum Proving Ground will include shared cryogenic facilities, labs, and space for private and academic researchers to collaborate. It will be used to test tenants’ quantum computing prototypes. DARPA’s director is Stephen Winchell. Winchell previously worked on Project Maven, a DoD initiative to integrate artificial intelligence–powered machine learning into the U.S. military. DARPA’s deputy director is Tyler McQuade. DARPA is funded through the DoD’s annual appropriations bill. Under Trump’s first budget, total national defense spending rose to $900 billion. DARPA also seeks private investment through its Commercial Strategy Office, which launched in 2019. The office has raised $1.24 billion in private capital from investors, including “family offices, angel investors, venture capital firms, corporate venture capital firms, and private equity (PE) or buyout firms,” according to the agency. It’s unclear whether DARPA will bring any new jobs to South Chicago. In an April meeting of the IQMP board of managers, the park’s CEO, Harley Johnson, said DARPA money is currently funding multiple IQMP staff positions. DARPA did not respond to multiple requests for comment from the Reader made by phone and email. Back to top IBM is one of the largest tech companies in the world and its roots in the industry date back to the 1880s. Since entering the world of quantum computing, IBM has created more than one hundred prototypes and has built a network of universities, industry clients, and quantum computing start-ups to move the technology forward. Its quantum computers start at one hundred qubits, much smaller than the one-million-qubit machine PsiQuantum hopes to build at the IQMP. IBM has chosen to make its software “open-access,” meaning anyone can sign up to use quantum computers, research, and courses for free, according to the company’s website. IBM will set up shop in the IQMP’s workforce development center, dubbed Quantum Works, where it will lead the National Quantum Algorithm Center in collaboration with U. Chicago. There, IBM will work with start-ups to “develop quantum software and explore algorithms and applications that could help unlock the potential of quantum computers,” according to a 2025 press release. As part of this work, IBM will also make a modular quantum computer, called IBM Quantum System Two, available to select start-ups for testing algorithms. The start-ups will be chosen through U. Chicago’s Duality accelerator program. IBM’s FutureNow Chicago delivery center will also be housed within the Quantum Works building, which developers hope to complete in 2028. The FutureNow hub is designed to bring industry professionals and local talent together in the areas of “AI, cybersecurity, data science, quantum, and more,” according to an April press release from Pritzker’s office. The company has created similar hubs in other cities across the world, including Bhubaneswar, India. Arvind Krishna is IBM’s chairman, president, and CEO. Krishna was also the top individual shareholder in IBM as of February 2026. Hanhee Paik, director of Quantum Algorithms Centers for IBM, is also a key member of the IBM Quantum team, which recently began working at the UI system’s new Discovery Partners Institute headquarters in downtown Chicago, according to a social media post by Paik. IBM’s National Quantum Algorithm Center is backed by a $25 million grant from the state of Illinois, according to a 2024 Chicago Tribune report. IBM is a publicly owned company and its top institutional investors include investment group Vanguard, BlackRock, and finance firm State Street, according to Yahoo Finance. In April, IBM signed a contract with the state agreeing to bring 750 jobs to the site by 2030 as part of FutureNow Chicago in exchange for an estimated $19 million in income tax credits. The new positions include at least 250 app developers, 150 software developers, 100 “account technical leaders,” 80 data analysts, and 20 corporate positions, according to the contract. IBM also agreed to hire qualified graduates of a new City Colleges of Chicago apprenticeship program called Moonshot for 180 of these full-time positions in an effort to hire locally for some of the jobs, according to a press release from Pritzker’s office. The program is just beginning, with funding from the state and the Chicagoland Workforce Funders Alliance, and will support five hundred apprentices over the next five years, according to a City Colleges press release. IBM did not respond to multiple requests for comment from the Reader made by email and phone. Back to top Infleqtion was founded in 2007 at the University of Colorado Boulder and was initially named ColdQuanta. The company plans to build a large-scale neutral-atom quantum computer at the campus in South Chicago. Rather than using particles of light, Infleqtion’s computers will rely on qubits of atoms trapped in space using focused lasers, according to the National Institute of Standards and Technology. Matthew Kinsella, Infleqtion’s CEO and founding investor, runs the company alongside cofounders Dana Anderson, chief science officer, and Pranav Gokhale, chief technology officer. The quantum industry has received some of the highest tax breaks in the state. Infleqtion receives about $107,000 in credits for every job created under an agreement with the state, according to analysis by the Illinois Answers Project. In total, Infleqtion’s work at the IQMP and the National Quantum Algorithm Center will include $50 million in public and private dollars, the company said in a July 2025 press release. The company also has a $20 million partnership with NASA to help send the world’s first quantum gravity sensor to space, according to a February press release. It received $2 million in funding from the U.S. Army to help deliver more accurate navigation and timing data and $6.2 million from the Department of Energy to use quantum technologies to optimize the energy grid. Infleqtion pocketed more than $550 million in a merger announced in September with special purpose acquisition company Churchill Capital Corp X. A special purpose acquisition company is a shell company created to raise money by selling shares on a stock exchange before merging with or acquiring a private company, which it then takes public. As part of the deal, Infleqtion began publicly trading shares on the New York Stock Exchange in February. Infleqtion’s state tax incentive agreement shows that the company will keep 14 existing jobs and create 36 new full-time jobs, paid at least 120 percent of the average wage of employees in Cook County in similar positions. Back to top Pasqal is a French quantum computing company started in 2019. Pasqal, like Infleqtion, specializes in neutral-atom quantum processors to power quantum computers. It seeks to build a quantum computer in South Chicago that would be the most powerful in the world, according to a 2025 Capitol News Illinois report. Pasqal is run by its CEO and board chair, Wasiq Bokhari, and chief technology officer, Loïc Henriet. Georges-Olivier Reymond, Antoine Browaeys, and Nobel Prize–winning physicist Alain Aspect spun the company off from an optics and photonics lab at the University of Paris–Saclay’s Institut d’Optique graduate school. Pasqal has received millions in tax incentives from a state program designed to support high-tech companies and manufacturers doing business in Illinois, as well as a $15 million loan from the state finance authority and climate bank. The company receives about $122,000 in tax credits for every job created under incentive agreements with the state of Illinois, according to a Illinois Answers Project report. Pasqal’s private investors include LG Electronics; Temasek; Wa’ed Ventures, the venture capital arm of the world’s largest oil and gas company, Saudi Aramco; Quantonation, a venture capital firm dedicated to investing in physics and quantum technologies; and Daphni, a venture capital firm whose mission is to build a socially and environmentally sustainable future by investing in “deep tech and disruptive innovations.” Pasqal must create at least 50 full-time jobs to remain eligible for state tax incentives, according to an agreement with the state. It has also committed to investing $65 million in its first U.S. location in South Chicago. Back to top Diraq is a quantum computing start-up founded in 2022 by Andrew Dzurak, an electrical engineering professor at the University of New South Wales in Sydney, Australia. The company was built on research done at UNSW and now employs more than 70 staff and students, with locations in Australia and the U.S. Diraq seeks to build a commercially viable quantum computer using a unique technology based on “silicon quantum dot spin qubits,” according to an IQMP press release. In 2025, the company partnered with Fermilab to develop ultraprecise quantum sensors. Dzurak is Diraq’s CEO, founder, and also serves on the company’s board. The board’s chairman is William Jeffrey, the cofounder of a “deep technology” venture capital firm, who previously served the U.S. government at DARPA and as the director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology. Diraq has received up to $1 million in federal funds through DARPA’s Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. The company received $20 million from the National Reconstruction Fund Corporation, an investment fund established by the Australian government to support key areas of the economy. Diraq will bring two to four jobs to the IQMP, according to the Illinois Answers Project. Diraq did not respond to multiple requests for comment from the Reader by email and through an IQMP spokesperson. Back to top Quantum Machines is a quantum computing company headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel. The company specializes in control systems used to operate quantum computers that more than half of quantum companies, including IQMP partner Diraq, rely on for their systems, a spokesperson said in a statement to the Reader. “Our technology enables researchers and companies to control and scale quantum computers, supporting research and future applications in areas such as materials science, chemistry, and optimization,” the spokesperson said. Quantum Machines is also working to build a quantum computer and world-class quantum research facility at Tel Aviv University called the Israeli Quantum Computing Center (IQCC). The project is funded by the Israel Innovation Authority—an arm of the Israeli government dedicated to investing in technology research and development. The IQCC, described as a “quantum datacenter,” is led by Quantum Machines alongside a consortium of partners, including Classiq, another major player in Israel’s quantum ecosystem; IQMP tenant Infleqtion; and Elbit Systems, an international defense contractor and military tech company based in Israel. Elbit Systems was recently awarded $200 million in contracts to supply Israel’s Ministry of Defense with airborne munitions during the joint U.S.–Israeli military operation launched against Iran in February. Quantum Machines was founded in 2018 by CEO Itamar Sivan, chief technology officer Yonatan Cohen, and chief engineer Nissim Ofek. Its founders met at the Weizmann Institute of Science, a public research university in Rehovot, Israel, where Sivan and Cohen both served as managing directors of an entrepreneurship program. As the newest tenant at the IQMP, signing on in February of this year, the full extent of public support received by Quantum Machines is unclear. Quantum Machines is not listed as a recipient of Illinois’s high-tech industry tax incentive program, but could still be receiving tax breaks through a special tax district created by the state. The Quantum Enterprise Zone program offers tax incentives to quantum computing companies located in, moving to, or expanding within Illinois that work with public institutions in disinvested areas, such as South Chicago, according to the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity. The Israel Innovation Authority and Israel’s Defense Ministry announced about $62 million in funding to help build its first quantum computer, an effort led by Quantum Machines, according to a 2022 report by the Times of Israel. Also in 2022, the Israeli Defense Ministry announced it would spend about $33.5 million more, when converted to U.S. dollars from Israeli new shekels, on developing quantum technology for military use. This momentum came from the broader Israeli National Quantum Initiative launched in 2018, the same year Quantum Machines was founded, with about $390 million in U.S. dollars in funding set to expire in 2024, according to the Times of Israel. A committee established last year to plan a successor program had not put forward a new plan as of April 2026. In 2020, Quantum Machines raised $17.5 million in private investment with the help of Israeli billionaire entrepreneur Avigdor Willenz. In 2021, Quantum Machines raised another $50 million from investors, including Claridge Israel, an investment firm focused on “Israel-related growth investments in innovative companies,” and Exor, the holding company of the Agnelli family, led by Italian industrialist and principal shareholder of Fiat Giovanni Agnelli. The fundraising push also garnered investment from Valor Equity Partners, the investment firm of American billionaire and U. Chicago alumnus Antonio Gracias, and previous investor TLV Partners, a venture capital firm dedicated to investing in Israeli entrepreneurs. In 2025, Quantum Machines raised an additional $170 million from private investors, including investment firm PSG Equity, Intel’s venture capital arm, and Israeli venture capital company Red Dot Capital Partners, according to a press release. In a statement, a Quantum Machines spokesperson said the company is “still in the early planning stages and have not finalized hiring numbers. As our hub develops, we expect to hire locally where possible.” Back to top The Reader is free. Producing it isn’t. And without your support, we can't continue. Title: BlackBerry Stock Pops Monday, Adding to Its Recent Rally. Here's What Has Investors Excited
URL: https://www.investopedia.com/blackberry-stock-pops-monday-adding-to-its-recent-rally-here-is-what-has-investors-excited-bb-11964990
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:55:12Z
Full Content:
Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise. BlackBerry's stock has been on a tear lately amid some signs of fresh growth. Could it be on its way to making a comeback? Shares of BlackBerry (BB) were up over 5% in recent trading, at their highest point in more than a year, following a report from The Wall Street Journal over the weekend pointing to strong growth in its QNX business that makes the software behind a wide range of driver assistance programs, among other things. Though well off their 2008 highs, Monday's move adds to a torrid rally for BlackBerry's stock this year, leaving the shares up more than 50% for 2026 so far. The report could boost confidence in a comeback for BlackBerry, powered by software rather than the hardware it was once known for. BlackBerry, which no longer makes phones, said in its latest earnings report last month that QNX's software is now being used in over 275 million cars worldwide, with the QNX segment accounting for nearly half of Blackberry's fiscal 2026 revenue at $268 million. QNX is also working to grow its reach beyond safety systems in cars, with its software also powering medical devices and other industrial machinery, per the Journal. BlackBerry shares have also benefitted in recent weeks from optimism around QNX's expanded partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), as the AI chip leader signed on to use QNX software alongside its own hardware to develop more uses for physical AI. Nvidia shares were down about 1% in recent trading, and have added about 5% since the start of the year. Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

$0.144274 $0.999456 $2,774.96 $0.126597 $0.193206 $1.015 $0.999726 $0.097919 $0.237422 $7.55 $1.00 $1,095.24 $1.68 $0.00000064 $4.29 $0.00204477 $0.151709 The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation will roll out tokenized securities trading in July with limited production trades, followed by a full service launch in October, bringing blockchain to its $114 trillion in custodied assets. The service will cover a defined set of highly liquid assets, including Russell 1000 constituents, exchange-traded funds tracking major indexes, and U.S. Treasury bills, bonds, and notes. Development has involved an industry working group comprising more than 50 financial firms across traditional finance and decentralized finance. Major Wall Street institutions participating include BlackRock, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Citadel Securities, according to a press release. The group also encompasses crypto-native platforms including USDC stablecoin issuer Circle and crypto exchanges Coinbase and Kraken, signaling broad industry support spanning both traditional and digital asset sectors. “As a global leader in financial services, DTCC continues to galvanize a broad cross-section of industry leaders to facilitate ongoing, robust dialogue that drives widespread digital assets adoption and advances innovation,” said DTCC President and CEO Frank La Salla, in a statement. “Our vision is coming to fruition: launching our tokenization service and successfully bridging TradFi and DeFi. We believe tokenization will significantly change how markets work and operate, bringing new levels of liquidity, transparency and efficiency to investors.” DTCC's Depository Trust Company serves as the backbone of U.S. securities settlement infrastructure, processing the vast majority of equity and fixed-income trades in American markets. The organization's massive custody base positions its tokenization service as potentially the largest bridge between traditional finance and blockchain-based securities trading. DTCC's tokenization initiative builds on regulatory approval the company received earlier this year. In December, the SEC approved DTCC's pilot program to record U.S. securities on select blockchains via registered wallets. The move follows broader Wall Street adoption of tokenization, with NYSE partnering with Securitize for tokenized securities and Computershare tapping the same firm to tokenize thousands of company stocks. “Tokenization is an important and critical step toward building tomorrow’s digital infrastructure,” said Nadine Chakar, DTCC’s managing director and global head of digital assets, in a statement. “DTCC is committed to remaining at the forefront of innovation and championing a scalable, interoperable and risk-managed Web3 ecosystem that harnesses the power of digital ledger technology and delivers real value to the industry.” Your gateway into the world of Web3 The latest news, articles, and resources, sent to your inbox weekly. © A next-generation media company. 2026 Decrypt Media, Inc.

Title: Micron Jumps 7%: Is the Memory Maker Now a Structural AI Winner?
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/05/04/micron-jumps-7-is-the-memory-maker-now-a-structural-ai-winner/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:28:18Z
Full Content:
Investing Micron Technology (MU) reported fiscal Q2 2026 revenue of $23.9B, up nearly 3x year over year, with gross margin hitting a company record of 75% and guidance for Q3 2026 revenue of $33.5B at 81% margin, driven by HBM supply constraints. Micron is positioned as the only American HBM supplier in a three-player oligopoly with SK Hynix and Samsung, capturing a structural shift where AI has recast memory from a cyclical commodity into a permanent strategic asset tied to hyperscaler AI infrastructure buildouts. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Micron Technology wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Micron Technology Inc. Shares of Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU | MU Price Prediction) are up 7% in Monday morning trading, changing hands at around $579 after closing Friday at $542.21. The move extends a parabolic run that has Micron stock up 103% year to date (YTD) in 2026 and up a staggering 618% over the past year. The session’s push has reignited a debate that has dominated semiconductor desks for weeks. Has memory permanently transitioned from a cyclical commodity business into a structural artificial intelligence (AI) hardware play? Micron sits at the center of that question. Micron’s fiscal Q2 2026 results crystallized the bull thesis. Revenue nearly tripled year over year (YoY) to $23.9 billion, with consolidated gross margin hitting a company-record 75% and non-GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $12.20. Fiscal Q3 2026 guidance calls for revenue of roughly $33.5 billion and gross margin of approximately 81%. Micron Technology CEO Sanjay Mehrotra framed the setup bluntly, asserting that “AI has not just increased demand for memory; it has fundamentally recast memory as a defining strategic asset in the AI era.” Mizuho’s Jordan Klein has called MU stock “crazy cheap” on buy-side numbers, with no new high-bandwidth memory (HBM) supply expected until late 2027. The bulls argue that Micron isn’t the same boom-and-bust DRAM (dynamic random-access memory) name it was a decade ago. HBM demand is locked into AI accelerator roadmaps at NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA), AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), and hyperscaler custom silicon, with combined hyperscaler capital expenditure (CapEx) commitments exceeding $500 billion for 2026 alone, including Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) at $190 billion and Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) between $125 billion and $145 billion. The HBM market is a three-player oligopoly shared with SK Hynix and Samsung, and Micron is the only American HBM supplier. Mehrotra noted that Micron can fulfill only 50% to two-thirds of key customer demand in the medium term, and the company sees data center bits exceeding 50% of industry total addressable market (TAM) for the first time in calendar 2026. That dynamic underpins Micron’s parallels to NVIDIA’s AI playbook. The skeptics aren’t gone. Memory has been cyclical for 50-plus years, and Samsung and SK Hynix are aggressively expanding their HBM capacity. There’s also AI CapEx normalization risk. Wall Street Journal reporting in late April on OpenAI cost concerns reignited questions about hyperscaler returns on AI spend, and HSBC’s downgrade of AMD on valuation today is a sentiment marker for the broader complex. This is where the debate sharpens. The bulls say Micron deserves a higher multiple because HBM allocations create recurring-revenue visibility that legacy DRAM never had. The bears point to a stock at a P/E ratio of 26x following a 600% one-year run and argue everything good is already in the price. Analyst consensus for Micron Technology stock sits at $551.40 with 39 Buy ratings against 5 Holds and zero Sells, while TIKR’s valuation model targets $664. Reddit’s tone has flipped sharply too, with sentiment scores climbing from the 20s in mid-April to the 78 to 92 range over the past two weeks. Investors should watch for whether Micron stock holds Monday’s gains throughout the week; they can also be on the lookout for hyperscaler CapEx commentary on upcoming earnings calls, and any analyst price target moves following the rally. HBM allocation visibility into 2027 and peer DRAM and NOT-AND flash (NAND) pricing trends remain the cleanest tells on whether the supercycle is structural or simply the loudest cycle yet. The bottom line: Micron’s blend of record HBM-driven results, raised Q2 guidance, and U.S.-based manufacturing footprint gives bulls a credible structural narrative, but the parabolic move and stretched valuation leave little margin for error. For now, MU stock remains a clean public proxy on whether AI memory demand has truly broken the cycle — and the next earnings print could be the verdict. Title: Wall Street giant DTCC plans tokenized securities platform with July pilot, October launch
URL: https://www.coindesk.com/business/2026/05/04/wall-street-giant-dtcc-plans-tokenized-securities-platform-with-july-pilot-october-launch
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:16:06Z
Full Content:
Major Wall Street operator Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC) said Monday it will begin limited production trades of tokenized securities in July, with a broader launch of its platform set for October. The service, built within DTCC's Depository Trust Company, will allow firms to issue digital versions of assets already held in custody, while keeping the same ownership rights and protections, according to the press release. The system is being shaped with input from more than 50 firms, including BlackRock, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and crypto-native companies like Anchorage and Circle, the firm said. The effort marks one of the most concrete timelines yet from a core piece of market infrastructure moving into blockchain-based settlement. DTCC sits at the center of U.S. markets, processing trillions of trades daily and serving as custodian of more than $114 trillion in securities. Tokenization — the process of representing assets such as stocks or bonds on a blockchain — has drawn growing interest among traditional financial institutions. Advocates say it can reduce settlement times, cut costs and open markets to new participants. "We believe tokenization will significantly change how markets work and operate, bringing new levels of liquidity, transparency and efficiency to investors," said Frank La Salla, DTCC President and CEO. DTCC's move comes as other Wall Street operators are pushing towards tokenization. Nasdaq is working on a framework for companies to issue blockchain-based shares and is partnering with the parent company of crypto exchange operator Kraken to distribute them globally, with a potential launch as early as 2027. Intercontinental Exchange, which owns the New York Stock Exchange, has also backed plans for tokenized stocks through a deal with crypto platform OKX, aiming to tap into its large user base. These efforts reflect a wider race to build what some call an "everything exchange," where stocks, bonds and digital assets trade on shared infrastructure. DTCC has gradually been building toward this moment. The firm has tested distributed ledger systems for years and has joined projects like the institution-focused Canton Network (CC). In December, it obtained a no-action letter from the SEC, allowing it to offer tokenization services for a defined set of assets, including Russell 1000 stocks, ETFs and U.S. Treasuries. Read more: Here is why Nasdaq and owner of NYSE are putting the $126 trillion equity market on blockchain More For You Michael Saylor proposes using bitcoin sales to support dividends, as Strategy reported a $12.54 billion Q1 loss. What to know: Disclosure & Polices: CoinDesk is an award-winning media outlet that covers the cryptocurrency industry. Its journalists abide by a strict set of editorial policies. CoinDesk has adopted a set of principles aimed at ensuring the integrity, editorial independence and freedom from bias of its publications. CoinDesk is part of Bullish (NYSE:BLSH), an institutionally focused global digital asset platform that provides market infrastructure and information services. Bullish owns and invests in digital asset businesses and digital assets and CoinDesk employees, including journalists, may receive Bullish equity-based compensation.

Title: Analysts See 27% Upside for Shopify After 30% Selloff
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/05/04/analysts-see-27-upside-for-shopify-after-30-selloff/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:08:11Z
Full Content:
Investing Shopify (SHOP) reported Q4 revenue of $3.67B (+30.6% YoY) with GMV reaching $123.8B (+31%), but net income fell 42.5%, and guidance indicates operating expenses of 37-38% of revenue as AI investment ramps. Shopify’s 66x forward P/E multiple prices in significant execution risk as the company invests heavily in AI-native commerce features embedded in ChatGPT, Copilot, and Google Search, with analyst consensus at $159.70 target implying 27% upside if margins stabilize and GMV growth sustains above 30%. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Shopify wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © 24/7 Wall St. Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP | SHOP Price Prediction) currently trades around $125.83, while Wall Street’s average price target sits at $159.70. This means Wall Street analysts, on average, see about 27% upside for the stock today. Shopify powers commerce for millions of merchants, from small businesses to large brands like SKIMS and Supreme. Its platform spans storefronts, payments, point-of-sale, capital, and increasingly AI-driven tools. The stock has long traded as a high-multiple bet on e-commerce growth, which makes the recent drawdown notable. The stock has sold off sharply, but the underlying business continues to grow at a strong pace. Shopify is down roughly 31% from its 52-week high of $182.19, a decline largely driven by profitability fears, with revenue actually beating consensus. The Q4 results were solid on the surface. Revenue rose 30.6% to $3.67 billion, beating expectations. However, net income declined 42.5%, raising concerns about profitability. Guidance added to the pressure, with management indicating operating expenses would run at 37% to 38% of revenue, with free cash flow margins in the low-to-mid teens as AI investment ramps. The selloff was company-specific, layered onto a broader rotation away from software names. Technical signals piled on, with one March note flagging a potential “Death Cross” as the 50-day moving average converged on the 200-day. Investors also priced in the risk that agentic commerce and AI-driven disruption could cannibalize Shopify’s storefront business. Despite the pullback, analysts have largely maintained their stance. Shopify’s Q4 GMV increased 31% to $123.8 billion, Shop Pay GMV rose 62%, and B2B GMV nearly doubled. International revenue grew 36%, and full-year free cash flow reached $2.01 billion. The bull thesis hinges on Shopify becoming the infrastructure layer for AI-native commerce. Recent product launches embed merchants into ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, and Google Search AI. JPMorgan reiterated Overweight with a $150 target, projecting 32% YoY GMV growth and 29% gross profit growth for Q1 2026, and RBC Capital carries a $170 target. President Harley Finkelstein framed the AI push as measured, telling investors the company is “leveraging our decades of experience to ensure that AI is carefully implemented”. The company’s $2 billion buyback program also reinforces management’s confidence. Of the 52 analysts covering the stock, 10 rate it a Strong Buy, 29 a Buy, 12 a Hold, 1 a Sell, and none a Strong Sell. Shopify trades at $125.83 against a consensus target of $159.70, implying roughly 27% upside if the sell-side is right. Coverage is deep, with 52 firms tracking the name and 75% rating it bullish. Year-to-date, SHOP is down 21.83% while the S&P 500 is up 4.7%, stark relative underperformance. Over a full year, SHOP returned 32.29% versus 30.59% for the index, a reminder that the recent drawdown started from an elevated base. Valuation remains the friction point. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 66 on EPS of $0.94, with a beta of 2.82. Analyst targets are one data point among many. The bull case rests on Shopify growing into its investments. If AI-driven features deepen merchant engagement, GMV continues compounding above 30%, and margins stabilize, the business can justify the current multiple and work its way back toward the $159 target range. But, you can’t forget that at roughly 66x forward earnings, the stock is priced for strong execution. If AI spending weighs on margins longer than expected, or growth starts to normalize, that multiple is likely to compress before fundamentals fully catch up. My view is that Shopify is still growing at a strong pace, but you’re paying a premium for it. Even after the sell-off, the upside for the stock may only materialize if Shopify proves it can balance growth and profitability over the next few quarters. Title: Nvidia’s Stock Plunged 90% Twice Before Becoming Most Valuable Company
URL: https://247wallst.com/personal-finance/2026/05/04/nvidias-stock-plunged-90-twice-before-becoming-most-valuable-company/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:02:17Z
Full Content:
Personal Finance Nvidia (NVDA) returned 221,833% since 2000, but a 90% drawdown requires a 900% gain to recover—a math that only works if you have decades of runway and can survive sequence risk. Are you ahead, or behind on retirement? SmartAsset's free tool can match you with a financial advisor in minutes to help you answer that today. Each advisor has been carefully vetted, and must act in your best interests. Don't waste another minute; learn more here.(Sponsor) Sending You to Google News in 3 © 24/7 Wall St. On a recent Motley Fool Money segment titled Nvidia’s Next Big Market, the host described Jensen Huang’s first 15 years running a public company in vivid terms: “If you look at the first 15 years, even as a publicly traded company, it looks like an electrocardiogram of somebody having a heart attack. The stock went down 90% twice.” The host added that many of Huang’s early bets became “$0 billion markets that to this day remain $0 billion markets,” and summarized Huang’s philosophy this way: “As long as one succeeds and you become the most valuable firm in human history, it doesn’t matter if the other ones fail.” That story gets repeated as inspiration. The stakes for a real investor are different. NVIDIA (NASDAQ: NVDA | NVDA Price Prediction) is now worth roughly $4.82 trillion, but the buy-and-hold-the-winner narrative skips over what those two drawdowns would have done to a portfolio that depended on the money. Huang’s approach is sound for an operator with decades of runway and control of the business. As guidance for a shareholder, the “just hold great companies” takeaway is incomplete because it ignores the arithmetic of deep drawdowns. A 50% loss requires a 100% gain to recover. A 90% loss requires a 900% gain. If you experience two separate 90% drawdowns and reinvest nothing in between, you need roughly a 9,900% return from the second bottom just to reach your original cost basis. NVIDIA delivered that, and then some. Most companies that fall 90% never come back at all. The historical record shows how violent the ride was. From the start of 2000 through May 2026, NVIDIA returned roughly 221,833% on a split-adjusted basis. Inside that line are two collapses the host described, plus a long stretch in 2002 and 2003 when the stock fell another 66% from $0.51 to $0.18 before any recovery began. The financial concept the quote points to is concentration risk combined with sequence risk. Time in the market only rewards you if you can stay in the market. A 58-year-old with $800,000 saved who put half into a single high-beta name watched that $400,000 become $40,000 during a 90% drawdown. If that happens in the first five years of retirement, no recovery curve fixes the withdrawals already taken at the bottom. Pull up your brokerage statement and add up every position tied to a single company, including employer stock and ETFs with heavy overlap. If any one name represents more than 10% of the money you will need within ten years, write down what your balance becomes after a 90% drop and ask whether your plan still works. NVIDIA’s fundamentals are strong: four straight quarterly beats, Q4 revenue of $68.13 billion, and analyst targets near $269. None of that changes the recovery math if you own too much and need the money too soon. Huang’s bet paid off because he had time, control, and nothing else to lose. Most investors have at least one of those constraints inverted. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Your wallet will thank you later. Title: Caterpillar Surges 190%—Analysts Say Hold for Now
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/05/04/caterpillar-surges-190-analysts-say-hold-for-now/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T15:01:39Z
Full Content:
Investing Caterpillar (CAT) posted Q1 2026 revenue of $17.41B (+22% YoY) with Power Generation revenue surging 41% YoY on data center engine and turbine demand, though manufacturing cost headwinds reached $710M and cash reserves fell from $9.98B to $4.07B. Data center power demand is driving Caterpillar’s momentum, but the stock has already priced in the data center boom, making further gains dependent on accelerating Power Generation orders and easing tariff costs in the second half of 2026. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Caterpillar wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Scott Olson / Getty Images The industrial giant has gone on a tear. Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT | CAT Price Prediction) sits at $890.11 after a blowout Q1 2026 earnings report, up 25.88% in the past month and 190.94% over the past year. Our 24/7 Wall St. price target for Caterpillar is $874.20 over the next 12 months, implying -1.79% downside. Our recommendation is hold, with high confidence at 90%. The stock is, in our view, fairly valued after a historic run. Our 24/7 Wall St. price target of $874.20 sits modestly below where Caterpillar trades today, and we want to be upfront: this is a stock with serious momentum. Real upside could come from accelerating data center power demand, where Power Generation revenue jumped 41% YoY, or from sustained infrastructure tailwinds. Treat the price target as one datapoint. A full bull case appears below. Caterpillar’s Q1 2026 results, released April 30, 2026, were the catalyst behind a 9.88% single-day surge. Revenue hit $17.41 billion, up 22% YoY, with EPS of $5.54. Construction Industries grew 38% to $7.16 billion, and Power & Energy climbed 22%. CEO Joe Creed cited “a record backlog” as the foundation for momentum. The stock now trades just 14% below its 52-week high of $896.98 after a YTD gain of 56.04%. Bulls have plenty to point to. Power Generation revenue rose 41% YoY on data center engine and turbine demand, with retail Power Gen sales up 48%. North America revenue jumped 32% to $10.23 billion. Caterpillar returned $5.7 billion to shareholders in Q1 alone, including $5 billion in buybacks. With 15 Buy or Strong Buy ratings on the Street, our bull case scenario points to $932.38 over 12 months and a peak of $943.71. ahtd / Flickr The bear case starts with tariffs. Manufacturing cost headwinds hit $710 million in Q1 2026, after $1.03 billion in Q4 2025. Resource Industries profit fell 39% with margin compression of 7 percentage points. Cash dropped from $9.98 billion to $4.07 billion, and dealer inventory builds raise pull-forward demand risk. Bulls would counter that the cash drawdown reflects aggressive buybacks, and that tariff pressure is an external cost issue. Insider activity has skewed toward selling. Our bear scenario lands at $696.91, a -21.71% drawdown. Our 24/7 Wall St. price target is $874.20, the recommendation is hold, and confidence is 90%. The decisive factor: the stock has already priced in the data center boom. The bullish path requires Power Generation orders to accelerate further and tariff costs to ease in the back half of 2026. The cautious path opens up if dealer inventory builds reverse or Resource Industries margins keep compressing. After a 190.94% one-year run, the setup favors patience over chasing the run. Looking further ahead, here is where our 24/7 Wall St. price target model projects Caterpillar could trade in the coming years, assuming current growth trajectories and tariff dynamics normalize. These projections assume Caterpillar continues executing on backlog conversion and data center demand. Significant upside or downside could result from a step-change in infrastructure spending or a sustained downturn in mining capex. Title: DTCC to run first live tokenized asset trades in July, eyes October rollout
URL: https://cryptobriefing.com/dtcc-tokenized-asset-trades-launch-july-rollout-october/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T14:38:41Z
Full Content:
Searching... The system, developed alongside more than 50 financial firms, will tokenize assets held at The Depository Trust Company. Share The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC), which processes virtually every securities transaction in the US and custodies more than $114 trillion in assets, announced today that it will begin facilitating live trades of tokenized assets in July, with a full commercial launch of the service planned for October. The initiative brings together more than 50 financial firms from both traditional finance and digital assets. Bank of America, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, BlackRock, and Charles Schwab are participating alongside crypto-native firms like Kraken, Anchorage Digital, Ondo Finance, and Fireblocks. The effort follows regulatory clearance from the US Securities and Exchange Commission. Last December, DTCC’s subsidiary Depository Trust Company received a No-Action Letter from the SEC allowing it to tokenize traditional custodial assets. The service will run on approved blockchains for three years and provide digital assets with the same legal rights and protections as their traditional equivalents. Covered assets include the Russell 1000, major index ETFs, and US Treasury bills, bonds, and notes. DTCC said the move supports an increased transition to digital markets, enabling innovations such as 24/7 trading, improved collateral efficiency, and programmable finance, while linking traditional and decentralized liquidity. “As a global leader in financial services, DTCC continues to galvanize a broad cross-section of industry leaders to facilitate ongoing, robust dialogue that drives widespread digital assets adoption and advances innovation,” Frank La Salla, DTCC President and CEO, said in a statement. “Our vision is coming to fruition: launching our tokenization service and successfully bridging TradFi and DeFi. We believe tokenization will significantly change how markets work and operate, bringing new levels of liquidity, transparency and efficiency to investors,” he stated. As of May 4, 2026, the real-world asset (RWA) sector is valued at around $25 billion, per DefiLlama. DeFi’s participation in RWAs totals $1.97 billion in TVL, while perpetual futures open interest is around $2.4 billion. The ecosystem currently consists of 163 issuers. In terms of composition, bonds lead by a wide margin at over $15 billion, followed by precious metals at $5.6 billion and private credit at $2.6 billion. Public equities contribute $838 million, with smaller but expanding allocations across digital assets and other categories. The market has shown steady expansion since 2022, with growth accelerating rapidly through 2024 and 2025. Title: Apple Hits Near-Record High, While Chip Stocks Face Pullback Risk After Massive Rally
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/05/04/apple-hits-near-record-high-while-chip-stocks-face-pullback-risk-after-massive-rally/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T14:07:10Z
Full Content:
Investing Apple (AAPL) trades at a 34x forward P/E versus its 10-year average of 23x, supported by $111.2B in March quarter revenue with double-digit growth across all segments, 49.3% gross margins, and a $100B buyback authorization, while spending only $6B annually on CapEx compared to hyperscalers’ combined $700B. Nvidia (NVDA) fell to $199 from a $213 record with a $4.85T market cap and 26x forward P/E, posting $68.13B in fiscal Q4 revenue up 73.2% year-over-year and guiding Q1 FY27 at roughly $78B, but faces emerging competition from Alphabet’s TPUs and Amazon’s Trainium chips. Both Apple and Nvidia’s stretched valuations hinge on whether their industry dominance is already fully priced in or if the market needs a prolonged pause before further gains. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Apple wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Canva The CNBC Halftime Report panel spent its May 1, 2026, show wrestling with two big questions that tech investors are asking: Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL | AAPL Price Prediction) closed the session within 1% of a new record high, trading at $280.14 after a 9.59% April rally. The Halftime panel’s debate centered on a stretched forward P/E of 34x against a 10-year average of 23x. The bull case rests on the $111.184 billion March quarter, which Tim Cook called “our best March quarter ever, with revenue of $111.2 billion and double-digit growth across every geographic segment.” Services hit a record, gross margins ran at 49.3%, and China revenue rebounded 28%. The board authorized a $100 billion buyback, detailed in the 8-K filed April 30. One caller defending the multiple said, “This isn’t just a stock. This is the zeitgeist of the modern consumer.” Another argued that “the iPhone sales being a little bit light, usually that controls the entire narrative. And the fact that that took a backseat to everything else is just incredibly exciting.” One of the most interesting points about Apple today is the company’s capital efficiency. While hyperscaler peers have poured a combined $700 billion into CapEx, Apple’s annual CapEx sits at roughly $6 billion, which affords the company more capital to fund its buyback. For those who are bearish on AI CapEx spending, Apple has been extremely conscious with their spend. Some might argue this will put them at a competitive disadvantage in the long term, but for now, the company’s capital isn’t being spent on projects with questionable returns. The host bridged to the topic of chips with a warning: “At some point, momentum runs out of gas, or at least you need to take a prolonged sit-down before you get up and run again.” Semiconductor names gained 60-70% in a single month. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) trades at $199, down from a $213 record, with a market cap of $4.85 trillion and a forward P/E of 26. Fiscal Q4 produced $68.13 billion in revenue, up 73.2% year over year, with Q1 FY27 guidance of roughly $78.0 billion. One caller said the pullback puts the entry “right in front of your face.” Another pushed back, arguing competition from Alphabet’s TPUs and Amazon’s Trainium means Nvidia faces “real competition for the first time.” That is a thesis shift worth flagging, especially with Jensen Huang positioning Grace Blackwell as “the king of inference today.” The Apple valuation debate and the chip pullback both point to the same underlying question: can leadership continue at these levels, or does the market need a pause? With both companies still near record highs, the question is whether industry-dominating performance is already fully priced in. Thomas previously served as a Content Lead at TIKR, a stock research platform, where he helped scale the company’s blog to hundreds of articles per month and contributed to a weekly newsletter reaching more than 100,000 investors. He specializes in breaking down complex companies into clear, actionable insights for everyday investors, with a focus on fundamentals-driven research. His work has also been featured on platforms including Seeking Alpha and Sure Dividend. Outside of work, Thomas enjoys weight lifting and soccer. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Title: Etsy, Amazon, eBay, and Shopify in April: Why E-Commerce Stocks Diverged So Dramatically
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/05/04/etsy-amazon-ebay-and-shopify-in-april-why-e-commerce-stocks-diverged-so-dramatically/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T14:06:44Z
Full Content:
Investing Etsy (ETSY) stock rallied 29% in April after reporting Q1 revenue of $631M (beating consensus by $13.7M) with marketplace gross merchandise sales growing 6% YoY and active buyers expanding for the first time in two years. Amazon (AMZN) stock gained 27% on AWS growth accelerating to 28% with a record $364B backlog, marking the fastest cloud growth in 15 quarters; eBay (EBAY) climbed 14% following Q4 revenue of $2.97B (up 15% YoY) and U.S. GMV surges. Etsy (ETSY) stock and Shopify (SHOP) stock lagged as investors focused on differentiated business models and category leadership rather than broad consumer sentiment. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Amazon wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Tero Vesalainen / iStock via Getty Images April 2026 produced a striking four-way split across U.S. e-commerce stocks despite a shared consumer backdrop. Etsy (NASDAQ:ETSY | ETSY Price Prediction) led the group with a 29% gain, narrowly edging out Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) at 27%. eBay (NASDAQ:EBAY) climbed 14%, while Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) barely budged at 2%. The same macro inputs produced wildly different outcomes, which tells investors something important. Differentiated business models, category leadership, and recovery optionality drove the month, not broad consumer sentiment. Etsy and Amazon both reported strong quarters on April 29, eBay rode prior momentum, and Shopify’s quiet month reflected real platform-side skepticism. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index sat at 53.3 in March 2026, well below the neutral 80 baseline. Even so, total personal consumption expenditures rose to $21,860.5 billion in March, with clothing and recreational goods at multi-month highs. Consumers kept spending while voicing pessimism, and that gap shaped the divergence. Etsy posted Q1 2026 revenue of $631 million, topping the $617.31 million consensus. Etsy marketplace gross merchandise sales (GMS) grew 6% year over year (YoY), and active buyers expanded sequentially for the first time in two years. CEO Kruti Patel Goyal asserted that she has “even more conviction in our focus, and confidence in our ability to execute and translate recent momentum into durable growth.” The smaller market-cap base amplifies positive surprises, and Etsy still trades roughly 68% below its 2021 peak. That recovery runway is something the mega caps simply don’t have. Amazon’s Q1 2026 report delivered Amazon Web Services (AWS) growth of 28%, with backlog reaching a record $364 billion. That marks the fastest cloud growth in 15 quarters and reignites Amazon’s place at the center of the artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure narrative. Operating income climbed sharply alongside it. Wall Street’s price target avalanche followed, including Raymond James raising its target to $280. A 27% monthly move on a base near $2.88 trillion represents an enormous absolute dollar move. Amazon closed April at $265.06, reasserting Magnificent 7 leadership. eBay’s Q4 2025 results, reported in February, delivered revenue of $2.97 billion, up 15% YoY, with non-GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $1.41. U.S. GMV surged solidly, and first-party advertising revenue jumped to $517 million. Susquehanna raised its eBay price target to $110. The pending $1.2 billion all-cash Depop acquisition from Etsy adds fashion-resale exposure once it closes in Q2 2026. Ryan Cohen’s surprise non-binding GameStop (NYSE:GME) acquisition proposal arrived as a late kicker, although most of that news broke after the April 30 close. eBay’s slower top-line growth still caps multiple expansion versus Etsy and Amazon. Shopify’s Q4 2025 was strong on its own terms: revenue of $3.672 billion, up 31% YoY, plus an authorized $2 billion share repurchase program. Yet the stock remains down 22% year-to-date (YTD) in 2026. Investors keep de-rating high-multiple Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) names across the cloud complex. Competitive pressure from Amazon’s vertical integration weighs on the small and mid-sized merchants who anchor Shopify. Capital expenditure (CapEx) and partnership disclosures may have weighed on near-term sentiment as well. Reddit retail sentiment was bullish at scores of 70 to 78 on r/wallstreetbets, but on very low activity, suggesting limited conviction. The takeaway is clear: specialty marketplaces with recovery optionality (Etsy) outperformed, while reignited AI growth narratives (Amazon) followed close behind. Established marketplaces with steady fundamentals (eBay) posted solid gains, and platform names with multiple compression (Shopify) lagged. Differentiated business models and category leadership are doing the heavy lifting in 2026. Reddit sentiment on Etsy stayed very bearish through the rally, hammering on traffic concerns even as institutional buyers stepped in. That contrarian setup helped fuel the move. Amazon’s community, by contrast, peaked early on the Anthropic partnership before declining to a 32 to 61 range on AI capex sustainability worries. Prudent investors should keep an eye on Etsy’s follow-through on the new GMS trajectory and on Shopify’s Q1 2026 results for signs of a floor. eBay’s Depop closing in Q2 and any GameStop-related developments could shape the next share-price moves. The May question is whether Etsy’s recovery story extends, or whether Shopify’s multiple compression finally finds support. David Moadel is financial writer specializing in stocks, ETFs, options, precious metals, and Bitcoin. David has written well over 1,000 articles for leading online publications, helping investors understand markets, income strategies, and risk. His work has appeared in The Motley Fool, InvestorPlace, U.S. News & World Report, TipRanks, ValueWalk, Benzinga, Market Realist, TalkMarkets, Finmasters, 24/7 Wall St., and others. With a master’s degree in education, David has taught at the elementary, high school, and college levels. That teaching background shapes his writing style: clear, educational, and practical. David has also built a loyal social-media audience by providing trustworthy financial content on YouTube, X/Twitter, and StockTwits. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Title: Blackstone wants $1.75bn for the first AI-era data-centre REIT
URL: https://thenextweb.com/news/blackstone-bxdc-data-center-reit-ipo/
Time Published: 2026-05-04T13:42:59Z
Full Content:
Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust will list on the NYSE under BXDC, targeting newly built data centres leased to hyperscalers. It is the most direct way Wall Street has yet packaged the AI build-out for public investors. For most of the past 18 months, the financial story behind the AI build-out has been a story about other people’s money. Hyperscalers raised, lenders syndicated, sovereign funds wrote cheques, and the resulting boom in data-centre construction was paid for by an opaque combination of corporate balance sheets and private credit. On Monday, Blackstone offered the public a more direct way in. Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust, a newly formed real-estate investment trust controlled by the world’s largest alternative-asset manager, is seeking to raise as much as $1.75bn in a US initial public offering, according to a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The 💜 of EU tech The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now! The trust intends to list on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol BXDC, with shares marketed at $20 each. Investors in the offering will receive an additional 1 per cent of their purchase in bonus shares. It is a significant moment for the AI infrastructure trade. Until now, public-market investors wanting exposure to the data-centre boom had a handful of options, mostly the established REITs Equinix and Digital Realty, the publicly traded hyperscalers themselves, and a thin layer of more speculative names. BXDC is the first attempt by a major alternative manager to package the AI-era data-centre opportunity as a vehicle that retail and institutional investors can buy directly. The vehicle’s mandate is narrow and deliberate. Per the prospectus, BXDC will target newly built, stabilised data centres valued at between $250m and $1.5bn, leased on long-term contracts to investment-grade hyperscalers. The geography is similarly concentrated: top-tier US markets where supply shortages are most acute, including Northern Virginia, the largest data-centre hub in the country, plus Ohio, Maryland, Phoenix, and Austin. The investment thesis, in Blackstone’s framing, is that the addressable stabilised data-centre market is heading towards roughly $1tn over the next five years, and that institutional-quality assets in this segment will produce equity-like returns with bond-like cash-flow predictability. Filings flagged by The Real Deal project annual property yields in the 5.75 to 7 per cent range, with rents escalating 2 to 3 per cent annually. Whether those numbers hold will depend on the durability of hyperscaler leasing and the cost of capital. For now, both factors look favourable. The fact that the underwriting syndicate, Goldman Sachs, Citi, Morgan Stanley, Barclays, Bank of America, Deutsche Bank, JPMorgan, RBC, and Wells Fargo, lines up nine of the largest US and European banks behind a single offering is its own statement about how seriously Wall Street takes the trade. Blackstone has been moving in this direction for some time. We wrote earlier this year on the firm’s $10bn debt financing of Australian AI data-centre developer Firmus, structured as long-dated infrastructure debt rather than equity. The pattern has been visible elsewhere too: Oracle’s $16.3bn Stargate-related financing, Pimco’s anchor role after US banks retreated from the deal, and the broader treatment of AI infrastructure as project-finance real estate rather than corporate technology. BXDC fits cleanly into that arc. It is, in essence, the public-market equivalent of what Blackstone’s private vehicles have been doing for two years. The REIT structure simplifies the tax treatment for retail investors, the bonus-share mechanism softens the entry, and the income-orientated profile is designed to attract a different buyer than the AI-equity speculator. Income, not narrative, is the pitch. There are reasons to read the BXDC offering with care. The first is hyperscaler concentration. The trust’s tenants are a small set of the world’s largest cloud providers, and the long leases that make the cash flows attractive are also what concentrate counterparty risk. If Microsoft, Google, Amazon, or Meta materially recalibrate their AI capex, BXDC’s portfolio is, by design, exposed to that decision. The second is valuation. TNW has noted before that the US equity market’s current CAPE ratio sits near dot-com-era levels, even as the leading AI-exposed companies are profitable in a way most 2000-vintage names were not. Data-centre REITs are, in effect, a way to participate in the upside without buying the operating companies; they are also exposed to the downside if leasing demand softens. The third is power. The reason data-centre supply is constrained in Tier 1 US markets is, increasingly, electricity. New developments are queueing for grid interconnection in Northern Virginia and elsewhere, and the value of any asset BXDC acquires depends, in part, on the durability of its existing power contracts. The prospectus discusses these constraints; what it cannot fully resolve is the underlying physics of the grid. The most informative thing about the BXDC filing is that it exists at all. Two years ago, the question was whether AI infrastructure could be financed at the scale the industry needed. One year ago, it was whether private credit and sovereign capital could absorb the supply. Now, with hyperscaler capex tracking towards $725bn this year, the question has become how to give public-market investors a structured, income-orientated way to own a piece of it. BXDC is one answer. There will be others. Whether $1.75bn is the right number, $20 the right price, and 5.75-to-7 per cent the right yield will be visible in how aggressively the order book is built and how the trust’s portfolio performs once it begins acquiring assets. For now, the AI build-out has, finally, a publicly tradable, dividend-paying address. It will not be the last. I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, (show all) I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, and change. I write about startups, venture capital, digital policy, and Europe ecosystem, with an eye on the larger story beneath them: who gets to build the future, who profits from it, and how Europe is learning to speak in a louder voice of its own. Before moving into senior editorial leadership, I've built my career for over +10 years across journalism, storytelling, content strategy, SEO, and digital publishing, with experience in SaaS, hospitality, art, and culture.
